title: MAN ON PAINKILLER ACCUSED OF DWI IN PARKS COLLISION
summary: Soon after a state trooper warned a man on painkillers not to drive, the man popped two pills, climbed back in his car and crashed on the Parks Highway, seriously injuring a couple on vacation from California and nearly hurting another driver, authorities said. Stephen Capers, 30, of Big Lake was charged with driving while intoxicated, reckless driving and three counts of assault. Capers had not been drinking, but blood tests showed prescription pain medication in his system, assistant district attorney Bob Collins said this week. A person who knowingly drives while impaired by drugs -- whether legal or illegal -- can be prosecuted for DWI. "It's uncommon, but not unheard of," Collins said. "Most people abide by the admonition not to drive." Alaska State Troopers said they got a call short…

title: PROPERTY TAX CAP AT A GLANCE
summary: Ballot Measure No. 4 in the November election contains two provisions that would drastically change property taxation in Alaska. IMPOSE 10-MILL (1%) PROPERTY TAX CAP * TODAY: The statewide cap is 3 percent (30 mills), though no local government sets its taxes that high. * WHAT CHANGE WOULD DO: Because boroughs and cities are both technically municipalities, each could charge up to the cap. Property in Palmer (Matanuska Susitna Borough) or Homer (Kenai Peninsula Borough), for instance, would be subject to a total of 2 percent (20 mills), while property in Anchorage and Juneau, where borough and city are unified, could be taxed only at 1 percent. * RESTRICTIONS: Unlike current state law, the initiative prevents any increase over the cap, including money to repay bonds for schools, roads or o…

title: EMPLOYER INDICTED IN 1999 CORDOVA AVALANCHE DEATH
summary: A Bellingham, Wash., engineering company and its president were indicted on manslaughter charges Tuesday for the death of a worker killed in an avalanche near Cordova last year. The grand jury indictment, handed up in Anchorage, charged Whitewater Engineering and company president Thom Fischer each with recklessly causing Gary Stone's death. Stone, 46, was operating a backhoe in a steep canyon about seven miles northeast of Cordova when a massive load of snow slid down a 2,000-foot slope and buried him on April 15, 1999. At the time, Stone was working on a hydroelectric project being constructed by Whitewater. Neither Whitewater officials nor their lawyer, Charlie Cole of Fairbanks, returned phone calls seeking comment. The company had hired avalanche forecaster Dave Hamre of Anchorage to …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Polar routes for airlines Commercial flights over North Pole save time, money The Associated Press (Published October 12, 2000) Ottawa, Canada -- Flying commercial flights over the North Pole would cut hours off the circuitous routes planes now fly between North America and Asia and save airlines hundreds of millions of dollars each year, according to a new study commissioned by Canada and Russia. The study found that it would be feasible to fly nonstop between such cities as Vancouver and Beijing or New York and Bangkok. It was done by Canadian and Russian aviation agencies and found that the project would need an investment of nearly $37 million to improve the air navigation infrastructure in the North. That would include training air traffic controllers and establishing communicat…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Bill would extend energy tax credit The Associated Press (Published October 11, 2000) Washington -- Alaska Sens. Frank Murkowski and Ted Stevens on Tuesday introduced legislation to extend a tax credit to promote more energy development. The Republican senators said the bill is important not only for the country, but will help promote development of billions of additional barrels of oil from Alaska's West Sak field in the Prudhoe Bay area. The Clinton-Gore administration for several years has been seeking to end the credit, which doesn't apply to any facility built after July 1, 1998, Murkowski said in a statement. The senator said it makes no send to end the credit when the country is 58 percent dependent on foreign sources of oil. "Doing away with the credit sends a direct signal t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Business People (Published October 9, 2000) Charles "Buck" Adams, vice president and financial advisor with Morgan Stanley Dean Witter, recently received certification as a Certified Financial Planner from the Certified Financial Planner Board of Standards. Mr. Adams is also a retirement planning specialist and an estate planning consultant. The American Society of Women Accountants has elected Danna Moser president of the organization. Moser is with the state's Division of Legislative Audit. Other new officers include Janice Bruchhauser, president-elect; Terry Bailey, vice president; Val Tangen and Marsha Carey, directors; Jan Matthews, recording secretary; Linda Day, corresponding secretary; and Theda Hall, treasurer. David Byrne has joined the sales staff of KBYR radio station, 70…

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Southern Living At Home Southern Living Magazine has launched Southern Living At Home, a home-party division, as a new channel for distributing its lifestyle books as well as a new line of gardening, home decor and holiday products, said Christina Hoke of Anchorage, the first Alaska independent consultant for Southern Living At Home. The new division expects to hold its first home parties in January and is now recruiting other independent consultants. For more information, contact Hoke at 428-3228. Copyright © 2000 The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: Make It Alaskan fest slated Make It Alaskan Festival 2000 will be held Friday through Sunday at the Sullivan Arena in Anchorage. The event will feature 260 booths of Alaska-made products and certified Native Alaskan handcrafts, said Steven L. Rouse, executive director of Make It Alaska Inc., a sponsor. Wholesalers can get a preview of products from 8 a.m. to noon Friday. The festival is open to the public from noon to 9 p.m. Friday, 10 a.m. to 9 p.m. Saturday and noon to 6 p.m. Sunday. Admission is $4, with children under 12 free, Rouse said. Copyright © 2000 The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)

title: Trailers may get the boot
summary: JL Properties Inc. plans to displace at least 40 trailers in a Midtown Anchorage mobile home park to make room for a 10-story office building next year. The Anchorage developer will offer financial help to residents forced to leave Plaza 36 Mobile Home Court if the company successfully rezones the property so it can begin the $35 million project, said spokesman Kevin Bruce. But some residents said they would rather be left alone. "I don't want to live in an apartment building and I can't afford to buy a home," said 54-year-old Dolly Swanson, a 10-year resident of Plaza 36. JL Properties officials were scheduled to introduce their project to the Spenard Community Council on Wednesday night and to some Plaza 36 residents, many whom first caught wind of it only this week. JL Properties wants …

title: BEARDSTOWN LADIES ARE BULLISH
summary: The only regret Ann Brewer has about playing the stock market is that she didn't start earlier. Carol Scott. Leslie Wiley, Virginia Wolfe, and Libby McKinney of the Alaska Gold Nuggets Investment Club discuss their stocks at a recent meeting. (Bob Hallinen / Anchorage Daily News) Investing changed the life of this 67-year-old member of the Beardstown Ladies -- those stock-picking sweethearts from central Illinois. Granted, not every small-time investor goes on to co-author five books, appear on Phil Donahue and find herself enmeshed in a world of controversy, the kind that sparks juicy headlines like, "Jail the Beardstown Ladies!" But beyond the hoopla that made her and the ladies famous in the 1990s, Brewer said investing has offered her simple pleasures, like money and friends. And she w…

title: FORT KNOX PROPOSES UNDERPASS NEAR MINE
summary: Fairbanks -- A mining company has buckled under public pressure and wants to build a $1 million Steese Highway underpass. Fort Knox Mine had proposed allowing its ore trucks to cross the highway about every four minutes to take boulders from True North, a mine the company wants to bring into production. "It was just an argument we thought we just weren't going to win," said Bill Jeffress, Fort Knox environmental services manager. The plan calls for ore trucks to run beneath the highway. The grade of the highway would not change at the crossing point. Road construction will occur this winter if government permits are issued by the end of October or beginning of November, Jeffress said. A bypass road would be built while work on the Steese is taking place, he said. The new underpass, to be b…

title: VIDEO CLIPS SHOW ALASKA'S VIOLENT SEAS
summary: Alaska's commercial fishermen know a storm doesn't have to be perfect to be perilous. Few have the opportunity -- make that the misfortune -- of seeing green-water monsters smash the bow. Swells and waves on the Gulf of Alaska and the notoriously nasty Bering Sea can make even ships seem pretty puny. Here's your chance to see it safely. The Coast Guard has a training videotape with some remarkable footage. We're posting two snippets here. Watch the video of Amber Dawn and the wave. • Modem users • High-speed users The first, titled "The Wave." It shows the 110-foot crab boat Amber Dawn plowing through horrible seas in the Gulf of Alaska, near Kodiak. It was during a 1988 crab fishery. The boat had broken down and its skipper took it to port for repairs. By the time it was ready to go again…

title: MINORITY CANNERY WORKERS CLAIM BIAS
summary: Seattle -- A civil rights lawsuit filed by minority workers in Alaska salmon canneries back in 1974 has returned to court for yet another appeal. The (now former) workers, mostly Filipinos and Alaska Natives, claim the two companies that employed them gave them worse pay, housing, meals and promotion chances than their white co-workers. The companies, Seattle-based Wards Cove Packing Co. and Dole Food Co., insist there was no double standard. "This court is no stranger to this case ...," plaintiffs' lawyer Abraham Arditi noted as he began his argument Monday before a panel of the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals. Arditi accused the companies of using separate hiring channels to target white workers for higher-paying positions in Alaska salmon plants and hire nonwhites for lower-paying job…

title: DON'T GET INVOLVED IN LIVES
summary: Question: Two months ago, two of my employees became roommates. This arrangement seems to be affecting their work. They've become very involved in each other's personal lives and one of them seems quite preoccupied with the personal problems the other has. What do you suggest I do before things go further downhill? My common sense says I should talk to both employees together because they both had a history of good work before they started living together. Answer: Your common sense may get you into a great deal of trouble. Supervisors can't afford to step over the line between supervisor and personal life adviser. As a supervisor you have the right to counsel your employees about any needed improvements in work quality and output. As supervisor you have no right to comment on your employee…

title: INSIDE ALASKA BUSINESS
summary: FINAL BANKRUPTCY PAYMENT Chugach Alaska Corp. announced that it would make its final bankruptcy-reorganization payment to creditors by the end of June. Anchorage-based Chugach, one of 13 regional Native corporations formed under the Alaska Native Claims Settlement Act, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in 1991. The company was wracked by bad investments in timber and fishing operations, and the 1989 Exxon Valdez oil spill. The company turned itself around by expanding into contracting and support services. FISHING EFFECTS GET REVIEW The National Marine Fisheries Service will hold two public meetings to review whether commercial Pacific cod fishermen off Alaska are "competing" for food with the endangered western population of Steller sea lions. The review is part of a broader inqu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Nations ink polar bear pact Alaska, Russia Natives get key role in harvest limits By David Whitney And Doug O'harra (Published October 17, 2000) After eight years of negotiations and cooperation among Native groups on two continents, the United States and Russia signed a treaty Monday that will govern the harvest of polar bears throughout the frozen ocean off Alaska's west and northwest coasts. Representatives from Alaska and Russian Far East Native hunting groups will help manage polar bears in the Bering and Chukchi seas. (Daily News file photo) The agreement would create an international commission to monitor and study the relentless marine predators in the Chukchi and Bering seas and then set a quota of how many bears could be safely hunted for subsistence by Natives in both coun…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Baby's illness stumps doctors Months of tests fail to reveal secrets of mysterious ailment By Larry Campbell Daily News Reporter (Published October 30, 2000) Sometimes the pain is so bad at night that Romeo Mailo-Johnson cries in his sleep. Theresa Mailo plays with her 16-month-old son, Romeo, at Children's Hospital in Seattle. (Photo courtesy Theresa Mailo) He cries when he sees the doctors and nurses in their gowns. He screams when they have to poke into the shunt embedded in his chest to give him drugs and feed him, since he won't eat. The 16-month-old's weakened immune system isolates him in his room at Children's Hospital in Seattle. His mother, Theresa Mailo, figures it must have made Romeo stir crazy a few nights ago when he wouldn't stop crying. He kept saying to the nurses: …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Air passenger's rifle fires while being checked By Larry Campbell Daily News Reporter (Published October 25, 2000) A passenger's rifle accidentally discharged while being checked in behind the Alaska Airlines baggage counter at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport last weekend. No one was injured, but the incident highlighted what federal officials say is a growing problem with firearms among checked baggage. The accident happened about 6 a.m. Sunday as airline counter workers were checking a passenger's rifle, according to Alaska spokesman Jack Walsh, in the airline's Seattle headquarters. "The passenger declared the weapon properly, filled out our form and said it was unloaded," Walsh said. "But when it was passing through the CTX (metal scanner), the ticket agents noticed t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Code clips business at Thrifty Barber Shop By Molly Brown Daily News Reporter (Published October 28, 2000) In the basement of his Fairview house, Don Goins will cut your hair for $13, and give you a "style cut" for $16. What he can't do is show off his red-, white- and blue-striped barber poles and awnings. The $5,000 awnings are now painted reddish brown and the poles are covered with gray garbage bags secured with duct tape. It's not his choice, Goins said, but the will of the "darned" city zoning enforcement officials who allow his home-based Thrifty Barber Shop to stay, but not the signs. "I'm losing trade like crazy," Goins said as he finished cutting what was left of a client's silver gray hair. "(Clients) call me all the time to see if I am still open after seeing the barber p…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Details cloud debates for, against Prop. 5 By Liz Ruskin Daily News Reporter (Published October 16, 2000) The citizens initiative to legalize marijuana, which will appear as Proposition 5 on the ballot next month, will do much more than legalize marijuana. And it's the details that have some opponents up in arms. Al Anders chats with Len Karpinski recently at the Free Hemp in Alaska offices in Spenard. Anders is chairman of the group, one of several around the state working to garner voter support for Proposition 5. Karpinski drafted the proposed law. (Mike Conti / Anchorage Daily News) "What they're asking for is the moon and everything in it," said Aaron Harrop, a substance abuse counselor who is co-chairman of the No on 5 campaign. Len Karpinski, the Anchorage man who drafted the …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Round and round Anchorage drivers will go (Includes video clips) By Elizabeth Manning Daily News Reporter (Published October 7, 2000) The roundabouts are coming. The roundabouts are coming. Will Anchorage drivers revolt, or will they adjust? The state highway department is planning an $11 million project to widen and rebuild Dowling Road, starting next summer. The project has been discussed for years, but now that it's getting close, attention and conversation is focused on two intersections where engineers plan European-style traffic roundabouts. Watch clips from the "Dowling Road Roundabout" video An introduction to modern roundabouts  High-speed users  Modem users The plan for Dowling Road  High-speed users  Modem users Reaction to roundabouts  High-speed users  Modem users …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Timber report bright Japan demand could increase By Paula Dobbyn Daily News Reporter (Published October 26, 2000) Changes in the Japanese housing industry could bode well for Alaska's ailing timber industry, according to a new report commissioned by the state Department of Community and Economic Development. The report, by the Center for International Trade in Forest Products at the University of Washington, says revisions to Japan's national building code, combined with shifts in consumer preferences and building styles, have opened a door for products made from Alaska's Sitka spruce, western hemlock and red and yellow cedar. "With all this shake-up, it presents opportunities for Alaska manufacturers," said Michael Johnson, state trade specialist. "We have what they want," he said. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | UAF SHOOTER GUNNING FOR A FOCUSED EFFORT UAF'S MULLOY SETS HER SIGHTS ON GOOD PERFORMANCE By Beth Bragg Daily News Executive Sports Editor (Published September 10, 2000) Riflery is a game of mind control, a sport where the calming of nerves and the dismissal of distractions has as much to do with success as the ability to hit targets smaller than a dime from 50 meters away. A focused mind is as important to a shooter as a well-sighted gun. Here then is how U.S. Olympic rifle team member Melissa Mulloy of the University of Alaska Fairbanks spent her final week in the United States before departing for the Summer Games in Sydney, Australia: For the up-to-the-minute news and results from Sydney, go to adn.com's Olympics site . With seven days to go, she took a test at UAF in an attempt …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | TRAIN DERAILS, SPILLS GAS HAY FLATS LOSS FIGURED AT 100-400 GALLONS By Don Hunter And S.J. Komarnitsky Daily News Reporters (Published July 13, 2000) An Alaska Railroad train derailed near the Palmer Hay Flats State Game Refuge on Wednesday, spilling an estimated 100 to 400 gallons of unleaded gasoline from one of nine cars that went off the track and tumbled down a steep embankment. Five of the nine were fuel tankers, but only one leaked, railroad officials said. Ernie Piper, a railroad vice president, said he closed a leaking valve on that tanker when he arrived about 90 minutes after the accident. A set of wheels from an Alaska Railroad tank car lie partly buried in the dirt at the site of a derailment Wednesday afternoon near Wasilla. Cars carrying unleaded gasoline toppled into …

title: The Anchorage Daily News (www.adn.com)
summary: The Alaska Native Health Board announced three staff changes. Lureta Whitewing-Porter was welcomed back to the HIV/AIDS prevention program as the training specialist. She has more than 17 years' experience in education, counseling, prevention and health promotion. Elizabeth LeBlanc was named to the Rural Alaska Sanitation Coalition and Alaska Solid Waste Management Program as the project manager. LeBlanc has more than 18 years' experience in land management, land use, community planning and project management. Nicholas Morgan was promoted to technical support specialist for the RASC/SWM program. Morgan will provide support to all activities of RASC/SWM programs. Morgan has more than eight years' experience in the health prevention/promotion field. Dana Hahn was promoted to manager of West …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Battle to save sea lion threatens vital state lifeline By Tim Bradner (Published October 8, 2000) For Anchorage residents who haven't been much beyond Palmer or the Russian River on the Kenai, Alaska's coastal fishing communities may as well be on the moon. But people in those towns are pretty jittery these days, and there's some good reasons the rest of Alaska should care. Putting it simply, our offshore fisheries may soon become managed by the courts instead of biologists and scientists. Communities from Kodiak to Unalaska may become like those Pacific Northwest timber towns that lost their base industry in the wake of the spotted-owl lawsuits. Remember that? It was also an Endangered Species Act lawsuit, just like this lawsuit case in Alaska brought by Greenpeace over Steller sea …
